coredata.dc:title: A step forward in the determination of oxidative stress biomarkers in saliva by miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction followed by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Peris-Pastor, Guillem
Chansud, Nutnicha
Bunkoed, Opas
Benedé, Juan L.
Chisvert, Alberto
coredata.dc:description: Bioanalysis has gained prominence nowadays because of its utility in the diagnosis, prognosis and response to treatment of diseases. Most specifically, saliva presents a high potential since there exists a correlation between salivary and blood levels of biomarkers, whereas its collection is easier and lesser invasive than blood. In this work, a high-throughput and sustainable analytical method for the determination of two biomarkers of DNA damage (8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyadenosine (8OHdA) and 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8OHdG)) in saliva is presented. For their determination, a rapid and high throughput technique as miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction (mSBSDME) has been employed. A magnetic sorbent synergistically combining the chemical properties of polydopamine (PDA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been synthesized and used for the extraction of the analytes. Good analytical parameters were obtained for both 8OHdA and 8OHdG in terms of enrichment factors (3.8 and 3.2), limits of detection (0.10 and 0.18 ng mL−1), and precision (RSD 3–13 and 4–13 %). Relative recovery values ranging from 83 to 119 % were obtained, thus demonstrating that matrix effects were negligible and external calibration employing deuterated bisphenol S as surrogate could be used. To demonstrate the utility of the developed method, saliva samples from five healthy volunteers and from three people diagnosed with type II diabetes were analysed. Results showed that the salivary levels of these biomarkers were higher in those people suffering type II diabetes than in healthy people. This work improves some of the drawbacks related to the sustainability of previous works dedicated to the same purpose.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioanalysis
Dispersive-based microextraction
Miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction
Oxidative stress biomarkers
Saliva

Study of the dilution of the saliva
Optimization of the extraction and desorption variables

This article is based upon work from the National Thematic Network on Sample Treatment (RED- 2022\u2013134079 -T funded by MICIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033) of the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities , and the Sample Preparation Study Group and Network supported by the Division of Analytical Chemistry of the European Chemical Society . N.C. was supported by an overseas thesis research scholarship for the 2024 fiscal year (Contract No. OTR 2567-001) from the Graduate School, Prince of Songkla University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the whole experimental procedure: (a) sample pretreatment; (b) mSBSDME procedure.
Comparison of the extraction capacity of the different tested materials.
Study of the dilution of the saliva.

(a) Response surface of desirability function representing the relation between sorbent amount and desorption volume and (b) triangular surface obtained through Simplex-Centroid design for the optimization of the desorption solvent.
Main analytical parameters of the proposed method.
Relative recovery values obtained for 8OHdA and 8OHdG applying the proposed method to saliva samples.
Results obtained by applying the mSBSDME method to real saliva samples obtained from different volunteers.
An overview on reported methods for the determination of 8OHdG and 8OHdA in saliva.

head.ce:title: A step forward in the determination of oxidative stress biomarkers in saliva by miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction followed by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: GICAPC Research Group, Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Valencia, Burjassot, 46100, Valencia, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: GICAPC Research Group, Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Valencia, 46100 Burjassot, Valencia, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla, 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bioanalysis has gained prominence nowadays because of its utility in the diagnosis, prognosis and response to treatment of diseases. Most specifically, saliva presents a high potential since there exists a correlation between salivary and blood levels of biomarkers, whereas its collection is easier and lesser invasive than blood. In this work, a high-throughput and sustainable analytical method for the determination of two biomarkers of DNA damage (8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyadenosine (8OHdA) and 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8OHdG)) in saliva is presented. For their determination, a rapid and high throughput technique as miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction (mSBSDME) has been employed. A magnetic sorbent synergistically combining the chemical properties of polydopamine (PDA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been synthesized and used for the extraction of the analytes. Good analytical parameters were obtained for both 8OHdA and 8OHdG in terms of enrichment factors (3.8 and 3.2), limits of detection (0.10 and 0.18 ng mL−1), and precision (RSD 3–13 and 4–13 %). Relative recovery values ranging from 83 to 119 % were obtained, thus demonstrating that matrix effects were negligible and external calibration employing deuterated bisphenol S as surrogate could be used. To demonstrate the utility of the developed method, saliva samples from five healthy volunteers and from three people diagnosed with type II diabetes were analysed. Results showed that the salivary levels of these biomarkers were higher in those people suffering type II diabetes than in healthy people. This work improves some of the drawbacks related to the sustainability of previous works dedicated to the same purpose.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The early detection of diseases is crucial to receive appropriate medical treatment in time and prevent the development of the illness. Thus, there is a high demand on rapid and simple methodologies for the biomonitoring of the population [1]. Biomarkers play a key role in bioanalysis since their measurement in human body can provide valuable information about a certain biological process [2]. To this regard, the search for specific biomarkers related to a disease and the establishment of their normal levels and cut-off values in different biological fluids are encouraged to answer one of the main social concerns.

Oxidative stress is a consequence of the imbalance caused by the excess of reactive oxygen species (ROS) with respect to the natural antioxidant capacity of the human body [3]. These unneutralized ROS can react with cellular constituents including DNA, protein, lipids, etc., thus resulting in cellular damage, which can lead to health problems, such as carcinogenesis and ageing [4–6].

8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyadenosine (8OHdA) and 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8OHdG), and their keto-enol tautomeric forms 8-oxo-7,8-dihydro-2′-deoxyadenosine (8oxodA) and 8-oxo-7,8-dihydro-2′-deoxyguanosine (8oxodG), are two reaction products from oxidative DNA damage so that the higher the presence of 8OHdA and 8OHdG in human biofluids, the higher the DNA oxidation. Different studies have shown that these biomarkers are related with some specific types of diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and various types of cancer [7–9], in such a way the levels of these biomarkers in sick people are higher than in healthy people [10].

Since salivary biomarker levels are highly correlated with blood levels, in recent years saliva has been a useful tool in clinical research [11]. Compared to blood, saliva has some advantages as its non-invasive sampling, and easy handling and conservation [12,13]. Consequently, the development of sensitive and selective methods for the determination of oxidative stress biomarkers in saliva is of high relevance. As these biomarkers are not highly concentrated, extraction techniques are required to preconcentrate them and to eliminate interferences from the sample (clean-up) [14].

Traditionally, the most employed extraction technique for the determination of 8OHdA and 8OHdG in biological fluids has been the solid-phase extraction (SPE) [15–19]. Taking into account the multiple advantages of microextraction techniques over conventional extraction techniques, such as the reduction of resources, sample and waste, and generally being quicker procedures, the employment of these techniques has increased over the years [20]. Microextraction techniques, such as solid-phase microextraction (SPME) [21,22], on-line in-tube SPME [4,23] and microextraction by packed sorbent (MEPS) [24] have been employed for the determination of 8OHdG and/or 8OHdA in urine. Only two articles report the determination of both biomarkers in saliva samples. One of them uses SPME, but long extraction and desorption times are needed [21]. To counteract these prolonged times, we recently developed an alternative method based on solvent-assisted dispersive solid-phase extraction (SA-DSPE) [25], taking advantage of faster extraction kinetics of dispersive techniques over static ones. In this sense, there was a significant improvement in terms of extraction time consumption (i.e., from 30 min to 1 min). However, further improvements are still necessary, as the SA-DSPE method requires time consuming steps, such as centrifugation to collect both the sorbent after its dispersion in the sample or the desorption solvent. Thus, to avoid centrifugation steps, magnetic sorbents are a good alternative because of its easy recollection by just applying a magnetic field.

Regarding the employed sorbents for the extraction of both analytes in saliva, only hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) polymers were used due to their optimal combination of polar and non-polar monomers [21,25], but low extraction yields were obtained (i.e., less than 2.1 % for SPME and less than 11 % for SA-DSPE), and none of them were magnetic.

Polydopamine (PDA) has garnered significant interest as extraction sorbent due to its abundance of hydroxyl and amino moieties, and aromatic rings, conferring it amphiphilic properties (hydrophobic and hydrophilic) [26]. Thus, PDA can interact with potential target compounds through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking [27], which makes it especially interesting for the extraction of 8OHdA and 8OHdG. It can be obtained easily from dopamine without requiring the addition of a crosslinker or initiator chemical due to its ability to self-polymerize in aqueous solution. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) also offer very interesting properties as extraction materials due to its capacity to stablish hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking besides of small particle size, significant surface area, chemical stability, and high-water dispersion [28]. They are zero-dimensional nanostructures having a plane size of less than 100 nm and thickness of less than 10 layers [29]. Because of their outstanding water solubility and modifiability in aqueous solution, GQDs must be immobilized on the surface of another supporting material in order to be recovered from aqueous dispersion.

In this work, a new composite material based on the synergistic combination of PDA and GQDs is used to overcome the low extraction yields of the previous works. To increase the extraction kinetics, miniaturized stir bar sorptive dispersive microextraction (mSBSDME) [30] is proposed as sample preparation approach, thus requiring magnetic sorbents. To this sense, cobalt ferrite magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) coated with PDA and GQDs (CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs) has been synthesized. Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) was selected as instrumental technique to achieve good sensitivity and selectivity.

8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8OHdG) from Cayman Chemical Company (Ann Arbor, MI, USA) and 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyadenosine (8OHdA) from Biosynth Carboshynth (Berkshire, UK) were used as standards. As surrogate, deuterated bisphenol S (BPS-d8) ≥ 99 % was purchased from CDN isotopes (Quebec, Canada). Chemical structures and relevant information are given in Table S1 at Supplementary Material.

For the synthesis of the magnetic material, cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O) and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) from Scharlab (Barcelona, Spain), citric acid from Guinama (Valencia, Spain), dopamine hydrochloride from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany), tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane-hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany), and ultrapure water (resistivity ≥18.2 MΩ cm) obtained through of a Connect water purification system from Adrona (Riga, Latvia) were employed.

HPLC grade ethanol (EtOH) from PanReac AppliChem (Damstad, Germany), LC-MS grade methanol (MeOH) from Honeywell (Seelze, Germany), and tetrahydrofuran (THF) and N,N-dimethylformamide from Scharlab were employed.
Nitrogen for the evaporation of the extracts obtained after the extraction procedure was obtained by a NiGen LCMS 40-1 nitrogen generator from Claind S.r.l (Lenno, Italy).
For the chromatographic mobile phase preparation, LC-MS grade acetonitrile (ACN) and LC-MS grade water from PanReac AppliChem, and ammonium fluoride (NH4F) 98 % from Acros Organics were purchased.
Those reagents used for the preparation of synthetic saliva are listed at the Supplementary Material.

A magnetic stirrer MX-3K (18 W, 0–3000 rpm) from Anzeser (Frankfurt am Main, Germany) and a 3D-printed support specifically designed for 15 flat-base glass inserts constituted the multiextraction platform employed in the mSBSDME procedure [30]. 400-μL flat base glass inserts (31 mm height x 4 mm i.d.) from Labbox (Barcelona, Spain) were employed as extraction vessels. Cylindrical NdFeB magnets (3 mm length x 2 mm diameter, 45 MGO) from Supermagnete (Gottmadingen, Germany) were employed for the dispersion of the material in the extraction procedure. For adjusting the pH during the synthesis process, a Basic 20 pH meter from Crison (Alella, Spain) was used. For the evaporation of the extracts under nitrogen stream, a Stuart SBHCONC/1 sample concentrator from Cole-Parmer (Staffordshire, UK) was employed.
Those instruments used for characterization of the sorbent are included at the Supplementary Material.

First, the synthesis of the CoFe2O4 MNPs by co-precipitation of FeCl3·6H2O and CoCl2·6H2O was subdued employing a modified procedure [31]. Specifically, 10.80 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 4.76 g of CoCl2·6H2O were dissolved in 200 mL of ultrapure water. Then, 100 mL of a 3 M NaOH solution were added dropwise while being continuously stirred for an hour at 80 °C. After that, the solution was cooled to ambient temperature, and the MNPs were repeatedly washed with ultrapure water and ethanol. Finally, the obtained solid was dried overnight at 80 °C.

The synthesis of the GQDs was done via pyrolysis in accordance with a slightly modified protocol [32]. First, 2.0 g of citric acid were heated in an oven for 30 min at 200 °C. Then, the yellow liquid was dropwise added to a 100 mL of a 0.25 M NaOH solution and magnetically stirred for 30 min. The obtained GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C.

Finally, a modified procedure was followed for the synthesis of the CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs [33]. Briefly, 0.2 g of CoFe2O4 MNPs were dispersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then, 5 mL of the GQDs solution, 0.6 g of dopamine hydrochloride, and 10 mL of a 0.25 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.5) were added. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 24 h. Then, the obtained solid was magnetically decanted and subsequently washed with ultrapure water and ethanol and dried at 80 °C for 6 h.

Individual stock solutions of 8OHdA and 8OHdG were prepared in N,N-dimethylformamide with a concentration of 1000 μg mL−1. Then, to obtain a 1 μg mL−1 multicomponent solution, an aliquot of each stock solution was diluted in ultrapure water. All solutions were stored at −20 °C. In each working session, a multicomponent standard solution of 100 ng mL−1 was prepared in synthetic saliva (elaborated as described at the Supplementary Material). From this solution, working standard solutions for calibration ranging from 0.5 to 10 ng mL−1 were prepared in synthetic saliva. This media simulates real saliva samples.
An individual stock solution of BPS-d8 was prepared in ACN with a concentration of 1000 μg mL−1. In each working session, a 350 ng mL−1 solution was prepared in ultrapure water.

Eight samples from different volunteers were analysed. Each volunteer gave their written informed consent to participate in this study, which followed the ethical guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki, and it was approved by the Ethical Committee of the University of Valencia (Spain) (Reference Number 1527082).

Samples were obtained via passive drooling and were stored at −20 °C to breakdown the mucins. In order to separate saliva from food and/or phlegm and precipitated mucins, samples were defrosted and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min before analysis. 350 μL of each supernatant were employed for the mSBSDME procedure (Fig. 1 a).

First, 7 mg of the CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs material were weighed into a flat base glass insert, and then, the neodymium magnet was inserted. After that, the preconditioning of the material was done by sequentially adding and removing 350 μL of EtOH and 350 μL of ultrapure water, stirring each for 30 s. To perform the extraction of the analytes, 350 μL of the pretreated sample or standard solution and 5 μL of surrogate at 350 ng mL−1 were added, dispersing the material for 1 min. Then, the magnetic material was retrieved on the stir bar and the donor phase was discarded. 350 μL of ultrapure water were added without stirring to remove sample leftovers from the sorbent, and then they were discarded. To desorb the analytes from the magnetic sorbent, 100 μL of MeOH:THF (1:1, v/v) were added, and the sorbent was dispersed for 1 min. After the collection of the material on the surface of the stir bar, the extract was transferred to a conical glass insert inside of an injection vial. A nitrogen stream was employed to evaporate the extracts to dryness (ca. 3 min). Finally, 30 μL of water were used for the reconstitution step prior to LC-MS/MS analysis. Fig. 1b shows a schematic diagram of the proposed method.

A Sciex Qtrap 6500 plus LC-MS/MS system comprised of a degasser, a programmable pump, an autosampler and a thermostatic column oven with a Waters C18 BEH (50 mm length, 2.1 mm I.D., 1.7 μm particle size) column was employed throughout the study.

For the LC-MS/MS analysis, 5 μL of each extract were injected into the chromatographic system. The mobile phase was composed of 0.5 mM NH4F aqueous solution (A) and ACN (B), employing the following developed elution program: 0–0.5 min, linear gradient from 0 to 15 % of solvent B; 0.5–1 min, linear gradient from 15 to 99 % of solvent B, held for 1 min; 2–3 min, return to initial conditions (i.e., 0 % solvent B) and finally held for 2 min. The flow rate was set at 0.4 mL min−1 and the column temperature at 35 °C. The total run time was 5 min.

The triple quadrupole MS detector operated in electrospray ionization mode (ESI), by multiple reaction monitoring (MRM). Specifically, positive polarity (ESI+, capillary voltage at 5.5 kV) was used to measure 8OHdG and 8OHdA, and negative polarity (ESI−, capillary voltage at −4.5 kV) was employed for BPS-d8. The temperature of the drying gas and the nebulizer pressure were 600 °C and 55 psi, respectively. The m/z precursor → product ion transitions for quantification and for identification, and the collision energies for each target compound are shown in Table S2.

Fig. S1 shows a chromatogram of a standard solution containing the analytes and the surrogate at 5 ng mL−1 and Fig. S2 shows a chromatogram of a sample from a volunteer suffering diabetes type II, both obtained after applying the proposed approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection and performance of the selected composite
ce:section.ce:para: Target biomarkers are highly polar compounds that present hydrophilic moieties (hydroxyl and amine) in their structures. Saliva is mostly water (>99 %), where the target compounds are comfortable, so their extraction is challenging. PDA was anticipated to interact with the analytes during the extraction though hydrogen bonding with those moieties besides π-π stacking with the aromatic rings. On the other hand, GQDs provide practical benefits such as their reduce size and high-water dispersion, among others. Similarly, GQDs can stablish hydrogen bonding throughout carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties and π-π stacking with the aromatic rings. For this reason, a magnetic material composed of cobalt ferrite MNPs coated with PDA and GQDs (i.e., CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs) was selected for the extraction of the target compounds. The CoFe2O4 core imparts magnetic properties, enabling easy separation of the composite material. Fig. S3 shows a hypothesized mechanism for the extraction of the target compounds. Additionally, compared to other carbon-based quantum dots, citric acid-based GQDs are smaller and have more carboxyl groups in their structure [34].

First, the extraction capacity of different materials was compared. For this purpose, bare CoFe2O4 MNPs, CoFe2O4@PDA, CoFe2O4/GQDs, and CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs were employed to perform the extraction of a standard solution of 10 ng mL−1 of the analytes prepared in synthetic saliva. Additionally, an HLB-type magnetic polymer (CoFe2O4-Strata™-X) used in previous works to determine other biomarkers in saliva [25] was also used for comparison purposes. The selected analytical response was the peak area of each analyte after the extraction procedure (under non-optimized conditions). The results are shown in Fig. 2 .

As can be seen in Fig. 2, the extraction capacity of bare CoFe2O4 MNPs was the lowest for both analytes, demonstrating that the extraction was not performed by the interaction of the analytes with the MNPs surface but with the coating (Strata™-X, GQDs or PDA).

The results showed that both PDA-based materials were more suitable for the extraction of the analytes than CoFe2O4-Strata™-X, obtaining from 2 to 5 times higher extraction capacity, and thus achieving better sensitivity. This could be due to the higher presence of hydrophilic moieties in the PDA-based materials that would boost the extraction of the analytes taking into account their hydrophilic character. The inclusion of GQDs increased even more the extraction capacity. However, CoFe2O4/GQDs provided a lower extraction capacity. It could be attributed to this material was not retrieved properly after its dispersion in water during the preconditioning step, remaining a dark brown solution and leading to material, and thus analytes, losses. The best extraction capacity was clearly achieved by CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs. Thus, the simultaneous employment of GQDs and PDA improves the performance compared when used both materials separately, thus showing a synergistic effect. It can be concluded that the extraction of the analytes is mainly due to their interaction with PDA, likely due to the higher number of hydrophilic functional moieties present in its structure which facilitate hydrogen bonding, in addition to π–π interactions (Fig. S3). The use of GQDs improves extraction as they promote the dispersion of the material, thus increasing the contact area for the extraction of the compounds, in addition to contributing with additional π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding through their carboxylic groups as hypothesized previously.

The adsorption capacity of the material was calculated. For this purpose, the instrumental signal of an aqueous standard solution containing 10 ng mL−1 of the analytes was measured before and after extraction. This experiment permits to obtain information about the amount of analyte adsorbed by the material in the extraction step. Individual adsorption capacities of 0.3 ng of each analyte per mg of material were obtained.

To ensure the repeatability of the synthesis of the chosen material, the inter-batch repeatability was studied to assure comparable extraction capabilities between batches. For this reason, three different batches of the CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs were synthetised as described in Section 2.3 and were employed for the extraction of a standard solution of synthetic saliva at 10 ng mL−1 of the analytes. As can be seen in Fig. S4, similar results between all batches were obtained, demonstrating a good repeatability of the synthesis.
Different techniques were employed for the characterization of the synthetised material. Thus, morphology, saturation magnetization (Ms), adsorption features, and thermal stability were studied.

The morphology of the CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs has been studied and compared with the CoFe2O4 MNPs employing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S5a and b, respectively) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S5c and d, respectively). As can be seen in SEM images, both materials presented a spherical-type shape, obtaining a considerable higher size for the CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs as a result of the coating. The TEM images showed that the coating adopted a core-shell structure, where the MNPs are in the centre of the structure and the PDA and GQDs are wrapping the MNPs. This structure is strong enough to carry out the extraction procedure without experiencing leaching problems.

Regarding the magnetic properties (Fig. S6a), a magnetization saturation value of 27.8 emu g−1 was obtained for CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs and 74.2 emu g−1 for CoFe2O4 MNPs. The diminution in the Ms value is due to the lower proportion of nanoparticles in the sorbent material. Nevertheless, this value is enough to accomplish the retrieval of the magnetic sorbent after its dispersion in the mSBSDME procedure.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere. As can be seen in Fig. S6b, a first loss of weight was observed from room temperature to 150 °C. This can be attributed to solvent or water evaporation that was remaining in the pores of the material. Since 150 to 580 °C a relative constant loss of weight was observed. This could be the progressive degradation of the organic matter. However, the most significant loss of weight was observed between 580 and 600 °C, corresponding to the final and total decomposition of the organic matter.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained for the synthesized material. Type-II isotherm was observed (Fig. S6c), indicating that the sorbent presents macroporous and an unrestricted monolayer-multilayer adsorption up to high p/p0 [35]. The results showed an average pore size of 52 nm, an average particle size of 800 nm, and a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 7.5 m2 g−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the dilution of the saliva
ce:section.ce:para: The dilution of the sample implies a decrease in the maximum theoretical enrichment factors, but in some cases, it is beneficial because it reduces matrix effects. Thus, a study of the dilution of the saliva was conducted to obtain the maximum sensitivity of the method. For this purpose, different ratios of synthetic saliva and ultrapure water (1:3, 1:1 and 3:1 (v/v)) and synthetic saliva without dilution, were studied for the extraction of the analytes. The concentration of the standard prepared in synthetic saliva was 5 ng mL−1. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , undiluted synthetic saliva gave the highest signal for both analytes. Thus, no dilution of the saliva was selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction and desorption variables

ce:section.ce:para: Different extraction and desorption variables that could affect the performance of the mSBSDME method were studied in order to reach the maximum sensitivity. The selected variables (and their ranges) were the ionic strength (0–10 % w/v NaCl, corresponding to added NaCl content to synthetic saliva), the sorbent amount (0.5–10.0 mg), the extraction time (1–10 min), the desorption time (1–10 min), and the desorption volume (20–100 μL). The value of the pH was not optimized as only 350 μL of sample were needed and it was not possible to measure the pH with accuracy. An alternative would be to add a buffer solution, but dilution of the sample would be done, achieving lower enrichment factors. A maximum ionic strength of 10 % was considered since higher values hinder a proper dispersion and further magnetic collection of the material because of the elevated viscosity of the donor phase. Regarding sorbent amount, a minimum value of 0.5 mg was established due to the high difficulty of weighting amounts under this value, and 10 mg were selected as upper limit because it was the maximum quantity admitted to coat the magnet used in the mSBSDME. For the extraction and desorption times, typical values employed in this dispersive-based technique were selected (i.e., 1–10 min). As desorption volume, a minimum value of 20 μL was selected to ensure a good dispersion of the sorbent in the organic solvent, and 100 μL were selected as maximum value to avoid high consumption of time in the evaporation step.

Due to the high number of variables, a screening study was conducted first to identify the significant ones. By this way, the non-significant variables can be omitted in the further optimization avoiding time consumption. To this regard, a Plackett-Burman design was performed allowing to identify simultaneously the impact of the selected factors on the analytical response (i.e., peak area) [36]. A small number of experiments is required due to the fact that the interaction between the selected variables is omitted. The statistical analysis was performed employing the software Minitab 19 (Minitab LLC, PA, USA) and considering the chromatographic peak area of each analyte as analytical response. As can be seen in Table S3, a total of 12 experiments were necessary. All experiments were performed employing 350 μL of synthetic saliva containing 10 ng mL−1 of the target analytes. The desorption was carried out with MeOH, and the extract was evaporated to dryness and reconstituted in 30 μL of water. This step was required to avoid peak splitting in the chromatographic instrument and to enhance the preconcentration of the analytes.

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) tests were employed to evaluate the results and Pareto charts were used to represent the main effects. Their influence is plotted by the size of each bar in the chart, and the vertical line corresponds to the 95 % confidence interval. Thus, when a main effect has a significant influence (positive or negative), its bar exceeds the vertical line. As can be seen in Fig. S7, the sorbent amount was a significant variable for both analytes, whereas the desorption volume was significant for 8OHdA. The desorption time was on the limit of the 95 % confidence interval for 8OHdG, whereas this variable was not significant for 8OHdA, thus it was not considered in the further optimization. Thus, a multivariate optimization employing these two variables was carried out. Regarding the non-significant variables, the minimum values were selected (i.e., no adjustment of ionic strength and 1 min as extraction and desorption times).

A multivariate optimization strategy based on the response surface methodology (RSM) [37] was chosen even if only two variables were significant in the mSBSDME procedure. A similar number of experiments (i.e., 9 experiments) for the multivariate optimization were needed compared to a univariate optimization. However, as sorbent amount and desorption volume are strongly related variables, a multivariate optimization was chosen to take into account the relationship between them. A RSM based on a two-factor three-level factorial design was employed. The studied ranges for each variable were the selected for the preliminary screening (see Section 3.4.1). All experiments were carried out employing 350 μL of synthetic saliva containing 10 ng mL−1 of each analyte, dispersing the material for 1 min, and using MeOH and 1 min of stirring as desorption conditions. Then, the extracts were evaporated and reconstituted with 30 μL of water. The selected analytical responses were the peak area of the analytes. The values of the studied variables for each experiment are presented in Table S4. For the statistical analysis, Statgraphics Centurion XVI (Stat Point Inc. Herdon, VA, USA) software was employed. Coefficients of determination (R2) higher than 96 % were obtained for both analytes, showing a good adequacy of the model. Response surface of desirability function was obtained. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, high sorbent amount had a positive effect in the extraction of the analytes. This can be explained due to the higher adsorption surface area available and thus higher adsorption capacity compared to lower sorbent amounts. 7 mg were selected as optimum value as the extraction capacity did not improve significantly for higher amounts. Regarding the desorption volume, higher values provided better extraction efficiency (EE). This can be explained as if high amount of sorbent is required to extract the analytes, large volume of desorption solvent is required to perform the desorption by ensuring a correct dispersion of the sorbent. When high amounts of sorbent and low desorption volumes were employed (e.g., 20 μL), the material was not completely dispersed, not allowing an efficient transfer of the analytes from the sorbent to the desorption solvent. Thus, 100 μL were selected as optimum desorption volume.

Desorption of the compounds follows a “like-dissolves-like” principle. A Simplex-Centroid design [38] was employed for the optimization of the desorption solvent. To set the optimum value, the model plots the triangle of all the possible mixtures of three components. The pure solvents are represented in the vertices of the triangle and the mixtures of them in the intermediate points. MeOH, EtOH and THF were studied as desorption solvents. Statgraphics was employed for the statistical analysis. Mixture fractions of the components were expressed as values from 0 to 1. The values of each solvent in the corresponding experiment can be found in Table S5. A set of 7 experiments were carried out and the selected analytical responses were the peak areas. The employed extraction conditions were the ones selected in the screening and the previous optimization. All experiments were carried out in 350 μL of synthetic saliva containing 10 ng mL−1 of each analyte.

The obtained coefficients of determination for the quadratic Simplex-Centroid design were higher than 95 % for both analytes, showing an efficient prediction of response by the designed model. As can be seen in Fig. 4b, MeOH:THF (1:1, v/v) provided the highest analytical response, and thus this proportion was selected for further experiments. These solvents have polarity indices of 5.1 and 4.0, respectively, whose mixture was effective at dissolving 8OHdA and 8OHdG, facilitating their efficient desorption from the sorbent surface.

The ICH harmonised guideline [39] was followed in order to validate the proposed method. Different analytical parameters as linearity, limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ), and intra- and inter-day precision (expressed as relative standard deviation, RSD) were determined. Furthermore, the enrichment factors (EF) for each analyte were also calculated. The results are summarized in Table 1 .

The LODs and LOQs were calculated as 3 and 10 times, respectively, the signal to noise ratio of a 0.5 ng mL−1 standard solution prepared in synthetic saliva and subdued to the mSBSDME method. LODs of 0.03 ng mL−1 and 0.05 ng mL−1 and LOQs of 0.10 ng mL−1 and 0.18 ng mL−1 were obtained for 8OHdA and 8OHdG, respectively.

The linearity of the optimized method was determined applying the mSBSDME extraction procedure to standard solutions prepared in synthetic saliva containing different concentrations of the analytes, employing BPS-d8 as surrogate. Calibration curves were plotted employing the area ratio of each analyte and that of the surrogate. The linearity was set at least up to 20 ng mL−1, obtaining coefficients of determination higher than 0.996. However, due to the low expected concentration of the analytes in the samples, a working range from 0.5 to 10 ng mL−1 was set. The obtained calibration equations were y = 0.03 + 0.40x (R2 = 0.996) and y = 0.02 + 0.15x (R2 = 0.997) for 8OHdA and 8OHdG, respectively, where y is the area ratio of each analyte and that of the surrogate, and x is the concentration expressed in ng mL−1.

With the aim of stablishing the EFs of the analytes, the mSBSDME method was applied to a standard solution containing 10 ng mL−1 of the 8OHdA and 8OHdG prepared in synthetic saliva. A standard solution of the same concentration prepared in the injection solvent (i.e., ultrapure water) was directly measured in the LC-MS/MS instrument. Taking into account the definition of the EF and that there is a linear correlation between the concentration and the instrumental signal (i.e., peak area (A)), the following equation was employed to calculate the EFs:
(1)
E F =
C
f i n a l
C
i n i t i a l
=
A
f i n a l
A
i n i t i a l
where Cfinal and Afinal are, respectively, the concentration and area of the analytes in the measured extract after the mSBSDME procedure, and Cinitial and Ainitial are, respectively, the concentration and area of the analytes in the aqueous solution not subjected to mSBSDME and measured directly. The obtained values were 3.8 for 8OHdA and 3.2 for 8OHdG.

The extraction efficiency of the method was calculated taking into account the experimental enrichment factor (EFexp) calculated employing Equation (1) and the maximum theorical enrichment factor that can be obtained (EFtheo), employing Equation (2):
(2)
E E
( % )
=
E F
exp
E F
t h e o

The value of the EFtheo can be calculated considering the sample volume (i.e., 350 μL) and the volume of the final extract (i.e., 30 μL), obtaining a value of 11.7. Thus, the extraction efficiency was 33 % for the 8OHdA and 27 % for the 8OHdG.

To evaluate the precision of the method, the RSD values of different standard solutions prepared in synthetic saliva and subdued to the proposed method employing BPS-d8 as surrogate were calculated. Three different concentrations (i.e., 0.5, 5 and 10 ng mL−1) were tested in the same day (intra-day precision) and in five consecutive days (inter-day precision). As can be seen in Table 1, the obtained values ranged from 3 to 13 % for 8OHdA and from 4 to 13 % for 8OHdG.
All these analytical features show the great potential of the developed method for its application to the determination of 8OHdA and 8OHdG in saliva samples.

The accuracy of the method was evaluated by spiking three saliva samples from different volunteers at two concentration levels (i.e., 0.5 and 5 ng mL−1) and applying the proposed method. Relative recoveries were calculated for both analytes, obtaining values from 90 to 119 % for 8OHdA and from 83 to 116 % for 8OHdG (see Table 2 ). These values showed that matrix effects were negligible. Thus, external calibration could be employed for the determination of both analytes in saliva using BPS-d8 as surrogate. Surrogate was added to correct possible deviations not only in the measurement stage but during the whole extraction procedure.

Different samples from 8 volunteers were analysed to assess the utility of the method for the determination of the 8OHdA and 8OHdG biomarkers in saliva. Volunteers 1–5 were healthy ones, and volunteers 6–8 present diabetes type II. As can be seen in Table 3 , the found concentrations of both biomarkers in healthy volunteers ranged from <LOD to 1.7 ng mL−1, obtaining higher concentrations (up to 6.0 ng mL−1) for volunteers suffering type II diabetes.

These results reveal that the developed method is suitable to determine these oxidative stress biomarkers in saliva and, although is not the aim of this paper and a high number of samples should be considered, the difference in the found contents reveal that it can be used to discriminate between healthy and type II diabetic people.

As said before, only two previous research articles reported the determination of 8OHdA and 8OHdG in saliva, a first one based on SPME and a second one on SA-DSPE, both of them using LC-MS/MS as measurement technique. A comparison of the sustainability (i.e., analytical performance, greenness, and practicability) between the proposed method and these previous works is shown in Table 4 .

Regarding the sample volume, the proposed method (350 μL) presents significant improvements, reducing the necessary volume 5–9 times compared to the previous methods. Even though saliva is a non-invasive sample and it can be obtained by passive drooling, high sample volumes may be difficult to obtain [11]. SPME requires 1.8 mL, whereas SA-DSPE requires 3 mL. Precisely the main drawback of the SA-DSPE method is the high sample volume required as a consequence of the employment of standard addition calibration to overcome matrix effect problems. This has been improved in the proposed mSBSDME method by the addition of a surrogate that corrects matrix effects, thus reducing the required amount of sample.

Moreover, in routine clinical laboratories, rapid methods are required to treat as many samples per unit of time as possible. In the previous works, sample preparation step was accomplished in 17 min for SA-DSPE (1 min extraction + 7 min desorption + 9 min centrifugation) and 60 min for SPME (30 min extraction + 30 min desorption). These values are considerably higher than the 2 min employed in the mSBSDME procedure (1 min extraction + 1 min desorption). Although this method requires an additional evaporation and reconstitution step, it offers a higher throughput due to the low time consumed in the extraction and desorption steps, favoured by the employment of a dispersive microextraction technique and a magnetic material.

Regarding analytical performance, an improvement in terms of LODs and EE have been achieved employing the mSBSDME method due to the good dispersion of the sorbent material in the donor phase and its interactions with the analytes, achieving better sensitivity.

The greenness of the sample preparation step was evaluated by employing the green metric tool AGREEprep [40]. The obtained values show that sample preparation step is similar for the developed approach and the SPME method, and greener than the SA-DSPE method. The developed method present improvements in terms of sample throughput and size economy of the sample, but the employment of MeOH and THF is a penalty point, although it should be said that low amounts are used. The values considered for each criterion are included in the Supplementary Material.

To assess the practicality of the analytical method, the blue applicability grade index (BAGI) [41] was applied. Values over 60 mean that the developed method can be considered practical. A score of 62.5 was obtained in the proposed methodology, improving the values presented by previous methods that were under 60 (Table S6). The most remarkable differences are the improvements in the items of simultaneous sample preparation, the samples per hour and the amount of sample. It is worth mention that this throughput can be considerably increased by the recently presented 96-position platform for mSBSDME [42]. The values considered for each criterion are included in Supplementary Material.

In the present work, a step forward in the determination of 8OHdA and 8OHdG in saliva has been achieved. The CoFe2O4@PDA/GQDs sorbent enhances the extraction of the analytes via hydrophilic, lipophilic, and π-π interactions. Moreover, a great throughput is achieved due to the employment of the mSBSDME, which, in addition to being a dispersive technique thus requiring low extraction and desorption times (i.e., 1 min), it allows the extraction of up to 15 samples simultaneously (which could even be increased to 96 samples if the new platform developed would be used). The proposed method has been validated in terms of linearity, sensitivity, precision and enrichment factors, obtaining excellent analytical performance. Relative recoveries between 83 and 119 % were obtained, showing that matrix effects were negligible and external calibration employing BPS-d8 as surrogate could be used. Moreover, the applicability of the method was demonstrated analysing saliva samples from healthy volunteers and people diagnosed with type II diabetes, obtaining higher values for the latest. The developed method was compared with previous works with the same purpose, improving in terms of sample requirement, sample throughput, limits of detection, extraction efficiency and practicability.

Guillem Peris-Pastor: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Nutnicha Chansud: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Opas Bunkoed: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Juan L. Benedé: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Alberto Chisvert: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
